event: Salome with the Head of Saint John the Baptist
symbols: severed head on a platter — the cost of speaking truth to power, the prophet silenced
prison bars visible at left — the imprisonment that preceded the execution
red wall — the blood and violence surrounding the death of the prophet
Salome carrying the head — the instrument of Herodias's revenge against the truth-teller
description: A horizontal panel showing a young woman with dark hair, wearing a green dress with bared shoulders, walking from left to right. She carries a wide shallow platter or basin containing the severed head of a bearded man. Behind her, a red brick fortification wall rises, with prison bars or a dark opening visible at far left. A distant landscape is visible at right. The woman's expression is composed, almost detached. The style is early Renaissance with clear outlines and careful detail.
theological_description: Botticelli depicts the gruesome aftermath of the beheading of John the Baptist as recorded in Mark 6:17-29 and Matthew 14:1-12. Herod Antipas had imprisoned John for denouncing his marriage to Herodias, his brother's wife — John had declared, 'It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife' (Mark 6:18). At Herod's birthday feast, Herodias's daughter (traditionally named Salome) danced and pleased Herod so much that he rashly promised her anything she wished. Prompted by her mother, she demanded: 'Give me here on a platter the head of John the Baptist' (Matthew 14:8). Herod, bound by his oath before the guests, ordered the execution. The head was brought on a platter and given to the girl, who brought it to her mother. John's death is a paradigm of the cost of prophetic truth-telling. He was the last and greatest of the Old Testament prophets, of whom Jesus said: 'Among those born of women there has not risen anyone greater than John the Baptist' (Matthew 11:11). His martyrdom prefigures Christ's own — the innocent killed by the collusion of political power and personal vengeance. John came 'in the spirit and power of Elijah' (Luke 1:17), and like Elijah who confronted Ahab and Jezebel, he confronted Herod and Herodias — and paid with his life.
